skill.name: shader-programming-glsl
skill.description: Expert guide for writing efficient GLSL shaders for WebGL, Three.js, and game engines.
skill.summary: Create, explain, and optimize GLSL vertex and fragment shaders for real-time graphics.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings are false positives from Markdown inline code and GLSL shader examples. I found no shell execution, network access, filesystem access, prompt injection, or malicious intent in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks in Shader Documentation
low_findings.description: The external command detections occur in Markdown inline code and fenced GLSL examples. These lines document shader syntax such as gl_Position, gl_FragColor, uniforms, varyings, swizzling, and shader helper functions. No Ruby, shell, or host command execution is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is Markdown documentation and GLSL source examples. I found no syntax that invokes a shell, Ruby backticks, subprocesses, or external programs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Security Keyword Matches in GLSL Identifiers
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword detections map to ordinary shader identifiers and function signatures. The content explains UV coordinates and a mainImage shader entry point, not command and control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is a shader tutorial with no networking or remote control logic. The flagged lines are graphics code examples.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection in Metadata
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detection points to the skill description metadata. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing implementation, encryption routine, or security claim appears on that line.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 only describes GLSL shader guidance. There is no evidence of DES, MD5, SHA-1, or any cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Keywords in Shader Examples
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance detections occur inside GLSL functions or shader best practice text. The skill does not collect host details, run discovery commands, inspect processes, or access system configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched lines are shader entry points and graphics advice. No operating system enumeration or data collection behavior is present.

content.value_statement: Shader development is hard because small math or pipeline mistakes can create blank screens or slow rendering. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused GLSL guidance for real-time visual effects.
actual_capabilities: Explains vertex and fragment shader roles in the graphics pipeline.
Shows how uniforms and varyings pass data through shader stages.
Demonstrates vector swizzling and GLSL vector math patterns.
Provides a simple raymarching example using a signed distance field sphere.
Gives performance guidance for branching, interpolation, and repeated calculations.
Helps troubleshoot common black screen shader failures.
limitations: Does not include a shader compiler, renderer, or live preview environment.
Does not cover every GLSL version or vendor-specific extension.
Provides examples, but host application integration still depends on the engine.
Does not validate shader performance on specific GPU hardware.

use_cases.title: Create WebGL Effects
use_cases.description: Build custom visual effects for WebGL or Three.js scenes using vertex and fragment shaders.

use_cases.title: Optimize Game Shaders
use_cases.description: Review shader logic for branching, repeated calculations, and data packing opportunities.

use_cases.title: Learn Shader Fundamentals
use_cases.description: Understand uniforms, varyings, swizzling, and raymarching through concise GLSL examples.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Shader Concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain the difference between a vertex shader and a fragment shader. Use simple language and mention common inputs and outputs.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Visual Effect
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate user designing an effect before writing shader code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a GLSL effect for a soft animated gradient. Describe uniforms, varyings, and the fragment shader logic I need.

prompt_templates.title: Debug a Shader Problem
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer has a blank screen or wrong colors.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my shader issue. The screen is black after adding uniforms. Give likely causes and a focused debugging checklist.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Shader Logic
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user improving real-time rendering performance.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this GLSL shader for performance risks. Focus on branching, repeated calculations, vector packing, and precision choices.

output_examples.input: Explain why my fragment shader shows only black.
output_examples.output: The response would check whether color output is written, uniforms are set, UV values are valid, and alpha is visible.

output_examples.input: Help me create a raymarched sphere effect.
output_examples.output: The response would outline camera rays, distance estimation, march steps, hit thresholds, normals, and simple shading.

output: Move constant calculations to the host application.
Replace avoidable branches with step or smoothstep where suitable.
Pack related values into vectors when it improves memory access.

best_practices: Start with the smallest visible shader before adding complex effects.
Pass changing scene values through uniforms and interpolated surface values through varyings.
Use smoothstep, mix, and vector operations to keep shader logic compact and predictable.
anti_patterns: Adding complex raymarching before confirming the basic vertex and fragment pipeline works.
Calculating constants repeatedly inside the shader when the host application can provide them.
Using heavy branching inside tight loops without checking the rendering cost.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps users write, understand, troubleshoot, and optimize GLSL shaders for real-time graphics.

faq.question: Does it generate complete WebGL applications?
faq.answer: It focuses on shader programming concepts and examples. Application setup depends on the chosen engine or framework.

faq.question: Can it help with Three.js ShaderMaterial?
faq.answer: Yes. The concepts for uniforms, vertex shaders, and fragment shaders apply directly to Three.js ShaderMaterial workflows.

faq.question: Does it support raymarching?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a basic signed distance field sphere example and can guide raymarching structure.

faq.question: Can it debug a black shader screen?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide checks for position output, uniform values, UV ranges, and fragment color output.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to use?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed file is documentation only and contains no executable scripts, network calls, or prompt injection.
